🟥 MISSING A DILG CHIEF I DIDN'T EXPECT TO MISS  

[An MCT Reflection]

Every time Jonvic Remulla faces the cameras as DILG chief, I feel something I never expected: I miss the DILG version of Benhur Abalos.

I like tracking performance. I follow numbers, patterns, and consistency of behavior. That is my basis for judgment. Not loyalty. Not brand. Not “love” for a politician. 

I look at crime rates, convictions, audit reports, trust ratings, and how someone shows up in moments of pressure.

By that standard, Abalos’ DILG performed. And Remulla’s DILG feels very different.

This is not a subtle campaign piece for a losing senatorial candidate. Abalos already lost in May 2025, finishing 16th with over 11.3 million votes, outside the magic 12, outside the Senate. This is about what the data and the patterns look like when you bother to pay attention.

🟥 WHAT ABALOS ACTUALLY DID WITH DILG  

When Abalos moved from MMDA to DILG in 2022, I did not feel anything special. Technocrat lang. Traffic, COVID coordination, then suddenly he is the “little president” of interior and local government.

And let us be clear. Abalos is not some underdog outsider. 

He is part of a dynasty. His father, Benjamin Abalos Sr., was COMELEC chair and long-time mayor of Mandaluyong. The Abalos name has been in that city’s politics for decades. He grew up inside the system. He benefitted from it.

But even inside that dynastic reality, his work at DILG shows up in the numbers.

On the drug front, DILG’s 2023 reporting under Abalos showed:
- 121,582 drug convictions in 2023, not just arrests.
- 27,968 barangays declared “drug-cleared.”
- 56,495 suspects arrested in more than 44,000 anti-illegal drug operations, with seized drugs valued at around ₱10.41 billion.

All of this sat under the BIDA program, “Buhay Ingatan, Droga’y Ayawan.” It was the Marcos administration’s version of an anti-drug campaign, marketed as “more humane” but still aggressive. Behind the branding were measurable outputs: cases decided, barangays cleared, drugs confiscated.

On crime, DILG’s own year-end numbers showed that from January to November 2022 compared to the same period in 2023:
- Focus crimes (the usual “index crimes” like murder, homicide, physical injury, robbery, theft, rape, and carjacking) went down by 8.44 percent.
- Non-index crimes (violations of special laws such as illegal drugs, illegal gambling, and other regulatory offenses) decreased by 7.32 percent.

Beyond police work, the “unsexy” parts of the portfolio were also moving:
- The Bureau of Fire Protection had 2,918 fire trucks in service or in the pipeline, and 1,475 cities and municipalities with functional fire stations.
- The BJMP enabled 56,679 persons deprived of liberty to vote in the 2023 barangay elections, a historic level of PDL participation.

On top of that, Abalos started laying down the next generation 911 backbone. Under his watch, DILG held an Emergency 911 summit, forged partnerships with telcos and tech providers, and began localizing the 911 system through pilot command centers in LGUs like Morong and Alaminos, with the goal of building a nationwide, tech-enabled emergency response network.

By September 2024, public perception caught up:
- Abalos was rated the top-performing Cabinet member under Marcos Jr.
- DILG was named the most trusted government agency, and the department was consistently getting unqualified opinions from COA, the highest audit grade.

Other offices have also received top COA marks on paper, including the OVP under Sara Duterte, but the public conversation around them is very different because of the confidential funds controversy and questions on how money was moved and used. DILG, under Abalos, did not carry that kind of scandal while posting strong audit ratings and public trust at the same time.

You do not need to admire the man to understand what those numbers say. For a while, the institution was working.

🟥 THE ALICE GUO PHOTO, THE QUIBOLOY POST, AND SERIOUS SLIPS  

Of course, the story is not clean.

In September 2024, a photo surfaced of Abalos with dismissed Bamban Mayor Alice Guo, taken shortly after her arrest in Indonesia. By that time, Guo was already the face of POGO-linked controversy and questions about national security. The arrest was a serious moment. High stakes. All eyes on the state.

Then Alice posts something almost kawaii, and that friendly-looking photo with the DILG chief starts circulating. The timing and the tone clashed badly with the gravity of the situation. It was jarring.

Abalos went in front of cameras and said, “Judge me not, it is just one photo,” insisting it was situational, not a relationship. Maybe the meeting had operational reasons. Maybe it was part of a chain of events. But the way it was handled in public did not land well. For someone whose strength was supposed to be discipline and systems, that moment felt sloppy.

The same weakness showed up in the Quiboloy saga.

By 2024, arrest warrants against Pastor Apollo Quiboloy were already in place from courts in Davao and Pasig for child abuse and qualified human trafficking, a non-bailable offense. Police mounted operations around KOJC compounds, cases moved, and pressure built.

When Quiboloy was finally cornered, Abalos broke the news with one short, all-caps social media post: “NAHULI NA PO SI APOLLO QUIBOLOY,” with a photo, but no details on where, how, or under what conditions he was taken. In the hours before fuller explanations came out and formal briefings followed, that vagueness became a weapon.

Quiboloy’s camp claimed he was not really “arrested” by the PNP, some local officers dodged questions on-air, and skirmishers online used the confusion to either mock the government or feed the opposite rumor that he was still untouchable. A few more lines of context, or even a quick follow up post, could have blunted that. Instead, the vacuum was filled by everyone else.

My first reaction then is the same reaction I have often now: whoever runs this social media account does not understand optics. In a landscape saturated with disinformation, half-announcements are not harmless—they are fuel.

It is possible to say two things at once here. The operations against Guo and Quiboloy were serious, high-risk undertakings the state had to pursue. But the way they were communicated, the tone, the missing details, those were real failures that opened doors for those who wanted to undermine the work.

🟥 THE EXIT THAT STILL DOES NOT MAKE SENSE  

Then came the decision that still bothers me. He resigned as DILG secretary to run for the Senate.

The DILG chief is, in many ways, a little president. You oversee the PNP, fire protection, local governments, jail management, peace and order councils, disaster response. You are not just interpreting policy. You are making it real down to the barangay level.

On paper, if you are topping performance surveys, leading a highly trusted agency, and getting clean audit marks, common sense would say: stay. Build on the work.

Instead, he left. He campaigned. He lost. He conceded. The job he was demonstrably good at simply ended.

I once wrote a study on that loss. I never published it. I was worried it would look like something else, like a defense brief for a candidate, instead of what it really was for me: a case study in how performance and popularity do not always convert into electoral power. 

The core question from that draft still sits with me. Why would someone walk away from a job they are good at, where they have real operational power, for a race they might not even win?

That question is why watching Remulla now keeps pulling my mind back to Abalos.

🟥 ENTER REMULLA: SAME OFFICE, DIFFERENT CENTER OF GRAVITY  

Jonvic Remulla arrived with a different kind of political weight. Multi term governor of Cavite. Member of a powerful family that has shaped Cavite politics for decades. Another dynasty. Another man raised inside the system.

So this is not a comparison between “dynasty” and “non dynasty.” It is dynasty versus dynasty. System versus system. And what each one chooses to do when given the same national portfolio.

Remulla’s DILG style is easy to recognize after a few months.

1. COMMS OVER EVERYTHING  

He clearly enjoys the camera. Since taking over, there have been frequent, long press conferences about fugitives, protest actions, flood control, rallies, ASEAN planning, and more. Transparency is good. Talking to the public is good. The problem starts when the press conference begins to feel like the main work, instead of a report on the work.

A recent critique captured this frustration by calling it “optics first governance” and reminding people that press conferences do not arrest fugitives.

2. BUNGLED ANNOUNCEMENTS AND “PA JOKE” GOVERNANCE  

During monsoon flooding in July 2025, when people were anxious about water levels and class suspensions, he used the official DILG page to post:

“Mga abangers, sarap ng bogchi ko. Sa kabusugan ay nakaidlip nang sandali. Oh eto na inaabangan ninyo.”

You could almost hear the record scratch.

People were flooded. Classes were in limbo. Local governments were scrambling. And the national interior secretary was talking about bogchi and abangers on the official government account. Netizens reacted. Even celebrities like Jake Ejercito and Jessy Mendiola called it out as insensitive.

Vice President Sara Duterte, who is not exactly known for being fragile about words, still had to remind him publicly that if it is coming from an official DILG account, it has to sound like an official DILG account.

Remulla’s response was basically: this is who I am. He apologized if people were hurt, but insisted he would not change his way of speaking because that is how Caviteños talk. President Marcos Jr. even backed him, saying what matters is that the announcement reached people.

So now we have a DILG chief whose communication in times of crisis has a built in shrug. If you are offended, that is your problem. It is just his style.

3. HELICOPTER OPTICS AND ENTITLEMENT VIBES  

Then there is the helicopter.

In September 2025, UP Diliman students and campus journalists documented a helicopter carrying Remulla landing in an open area along University Avenue. Police and firefighters were seen escorting him inside campus.

An earlier letter to UP reportedly said there would be no uniformed personnel or marked vehicles involved. On the ground, witnesses saw the opposite. Escorts. Official vehicles. A very visible show of the state arriving for one man.

Online, people did not have to work hard to make the point. How do you lecture the public about traffic, discipline, and flood control, then treat a major state university like your private helipad in the middle of Metro Manila traffic?

To be fair, use of helicopters for cabinet officials is not automatically scandalous. When a PNP chopper crashed in 2022 on its way to pick up then PNP chief Dionardo Carlos from Balesin, DILG Secretary Año defended the use of the chopper, saying it is rightful for the PNP chief to use PNP air assets for official functions when there are no other means. There are rules. There is such a thing as administrative flights.

But legality and judgment are different questions. In a city drowning in congestion, in a public university with its own history of state violence and protest, a flashy chopper drop off reads as entitlement, even if the paperwork is in order.

4. PRESSCONS VS OUTCOMES: FUGITIVES STILL FREE  

Now look at the flood control scandal.

Remulla has been very visible in front of microphones, explaining where fugitives are and what the government plans to do.

- He told media they are “pretty certain” that fugitive ex lawmaker Zaldy Co is in a gated community in Lisbon, Portugal, with a Portuguese passport and no extradition treaty to lean on.
- He said he is willing to fly there to personally fetch Co as long as Co is willing to return.
- On Atong Ang, he has said in separate interviews that the government believes Ang is still in the Philippines, that they have conducted multiple raids, and that he has ties to NBI officials under investigation.

All that looks serious on camera. It sounds decisive. It fills airtime.

But here is the uncomfortable scoreboard. As of now, Co is still abroad, Ang is still at large, Bantag is still reported to be hiding in Cordillera. Exactly what critics mean when they say press conferences do not arrest fugitives.

5. SOFT LAUNCHING 2028 FROM THE DILG PODIUM  

Then we have the 2028 conversations.

In several ANC and ABS CBN interviews, Remulla has said openly that a 2028 presidential run is a “possibility.” He has even floated the idea that the race should be a one on one contest. Again, this is not rumor. These are his own words.

At the same time, Ombudsman Boying Remulla, his brother, has said he does not plan to resign even if Jonvic decides to run. The message is clear. The clan sees this season as positioning time.

You add that to the helicopter story, the flood control briefings, the Trillion Peso March crowd management, the class suspension “jokes,” and what you see is not just a DILG at work. You see a national stage being used to test lines, build recall, and project image.

6. 911 AS A CONTINUED PROJECT  

On 911, he inherited a system that was already being modernized under Abalos and is now scaling it. Under Remulla, DILG talks about a “revamped” and “unified” 911: integrating local hotlines into a single system, boosting the national 911 call center that now handles millions of calls, and promising more fire trucks, police cars, and emergency vehicles so that those calls translate to real help on the ground.

That matters. But it also matters to say plainly: he is continuing and expanding a direction that was set before he arrived, not inventing it out of thin air.

🟥 WHY I STILL MISS ABALOS  

So here I am, watching Remulla do his thing. Facing rallies. Cracking jokes. Insisting there will be no VIP treatment for Bong Revilla. Talking about Lisbon. Hinting at 2028.

And I find myself missing what Abalos’ tenure represented.

I do not miss Abalos because he was flawless. He wasn’t. That Alice Guo photo and the way it was handled, and that bare Quiboloy post, are still part of his record. I do not miss him because I want him back in power. That is not the point.

I miss him because of what his DILG looked like in the data:

- Crime indicators moving downward.
- Drug cases that did not stop at arrest, but ended in conviction.
- Fire trucks and stations increasing across the country.
- PDLs being allowed to participate in elections.
- Public trust for the agency going up.
- COA giving the department clean opinions.

I miss a version of DILG that behaved like an institution focused on work, numbers, and systems, even if it was still led by a dynastic figure inside a flawed political landscape.

By contrast, Remulla’s DILG, at least so far, often feels like governance built around press conferences and personal style. 

The story is about him, his way of talking, his helicopter, his possible 2028 run, more than it is about institutional metrics and long term reforms.

Maybe that will change. Maybe the 911 upgrades and police mobility schemes he keeps describing will appear in the statistics. 

Maybe under him, crime will fall further and these fugitives will no longer be talking points but actual arrests.

I am open to that possibility. I track performance. If the numbers shift, my assessment will too.

But until that happens, I sit with this uneasy truth.

Sometimes you miss a version of institutional leadership not because you were ever emotionally invested in the leader, but because you saw the work move in the right direction. 

And when the next person, also dynastic, also powerful, steps into the same office and treats it like a stage, you cannot help but ask yourself a simple, stubborn question.

What would things look like today if the one who performed had chosen to stay and keep working, instead of chasing another title?

Sorry pero natawa ako when I saw who was filing the impeachment case against BBM. 

All I could see during the the presscon kahapon were crocodiles, and that's probably my issue for being myopic. But let me tell you why.

🟥 THE: WHO SIGNED THE COMPLAINT

The third impeachment complaint was pushed by a small bloc of familiar names: former lawmakers Mike Defensor and Jing Paras, former Ilocos Sur governor Chavit Singson, lawyers Ferdinand Topacio and Manuelito Luna, plus a few allies like Harold Respicio and Mary "Cathy" Binag. 

They accused Marcos of almost everything short of treason and direct bribery: culpable violation of the Constitution, betrayal of public trust, graft and corruption, and "high crimes," anchored on budget insertions, the so-called BBM parametric formula, Malampaya renewal, alleged drug use, and even "surrendering" Duterte to the ICC.

The House Secretary General's office refused to receive their complaint; instead of leaving it (like Makabayan did), they took it back and walked. 

So who are they, if you trace them not through their press cons, but through their case histories?

🟥  MIKE DEFENSOR: NBN–ZTE, MINING, IVERMECTIN, ABS–CBN, GUTEZA

In the NBN–ZTE scandal, Jun Lozada testified that he was abducted when he came home from Hong Kong, then visited by then–presidential chief of staff Mike Defensor, who allegedly offered him P50,000 to deny he'd been kidnapped and to clear Malacañang. 

Instead of helping investigate the ZTE deal, Defensor filed a perjury case against Lozada; a Manila court issued an arrest warrant against the whistleblower, while the officials he implicated stayed untouched.

As DENR chief, Defensor reversed the cancellation of the Mindoro Nickel Project in 2005, bringing back a mine previously junked over watershed risks, lack of LGU and IP consent, and its location on fault lines. 

He did it with no fresh public hearings; Mindoro LGUs later passed resolutions denying they had ever given the endorsements he cited, and people's orgs called for a Senate review of mining projects he approved.

After leaving DENR, Defensor surfaced as chair or top executive in mining firms like Pax Libera Mining, NiHAO Mineral Resources, and Geograce Resources, which later landed a US$150-million nickel processing deal with a Chinese group, raising constitutional questions on foreign participation in minerals. 

Legislators Satur Ocampo and Teddy Casiño filed a resolution seeking a probe into Defensor's mining deals and possible violations of the Constitution and mining laws; separate reporting tied his quarry interests to flooding concerns in Rizal.

During COVID-19, Defensor turned into one of the loudest political promoters of ivermectin, distributing it in Quezon City even as WHO, DOH, and FDA said there was no solid evidence it worked against COVID-19 and no product was registered for that use in the country. 

Facebook later blocked his account for medical misinformation; health professionals warned his messaging risked undermining vaccines and proper treatment.

In 2020, he was a lead face in the House move that killed ABS-CBN's franchise, calling the denial "not death penalty, but life imprisonment" for the network. 

The shutdown, widely seen as political retaliation, wiped out thousands of media jobs and shrank independent news space; it helped fuel voter backlash that cost him his 2022 race.

In late 2025, as flood-control hearings heated up, Defensor accused the NBI of illegally detaining and torturing an aide of Rep. Zaldy Co; the NBI publicly denied this as baseless, flipping the narrative back on him. 

Around the same period, he insisted a key "whistleblower," retired Marine Sgt. Orly Guteza, was under Marine custody; the Philippine Navy issued its own statement saying Guteza was not in their custody, contradicting Defensor directly.

Guteza, whom Defensor helped present as a "surprise witness" with Sen. Rodante Marcoleta in flood-control hearings, claimed to have delivered bags of cash from contractors to politicians like Martin Romualdez and Zaldy Co. 

Senators and lawyers later flagged serious problems with Guteza's affidavit, including notarial details that didn't match official records, prompting questions about falsification; as scrutiny grew, Guteza's whereabouts became murky, with conflicting accounts from Defensor and the military about who was actually "protecting" him.

This is the man signing an impeachment complaint built on alleged unconstitutional budgets, ghost projects, and abuse of power.

🟥  CHAVIT SINGSON: JUETENG, VIOLENCE, PLUNDER

In the Estrada jueteng scandal, Singson testified he personally collected around P545 million in jueteng money, stating that about P400 million of that went to then President Joseph Estrada. He described himself as Estrada's gambling "operator" and bagman, central to the illegal payoff system that eventually triggered EDSA II.

A Napolcom report later linked Singson himself to illegal gambling operations in Eastern Visayas, naming his group in alleged masiao (jueteng-style) activities and prompting an Ombudsman probe, which he denied. The pattern: deeply embedded in the same illegal gambling ecosystem he helped expose — shielded by immunity in exchange for his testimony against Estrada.

In 2009, his private life went public when his long-time partner Che Tiongson filed a case for domestic violence and physical abuse; court records note a petition before the Quezon City RTC and that she was placed in a safe house. 

Singson went on radio and admitted he had his partner and her alleged lover held and beaten, saying he did not regret it, that they were "lying," and that they were "lucky" he did not kill them. 

Women's rights advocates, including lawmakers, condemned his statements as normalizing violence against women, while he accused his partner of using the issue for extortion; despite the public admission, he continued in government and business with little consequence.

More recently, Singson has been hit with plunder complaints over an allegedly overpriced land deal in Narvacan, with accusations that the municipality paid around P100 million more than the zonal value while he was mayor. 

At the same time, he has filed graft cases against other Ilocos Sur officials for alleged misuse of tobacco funds, using corruption cases as a tool even as he faces his own.

This is the man standing beside Defensor, accusing Marcos of "graft and corruption" and "high crimes."

🟥  JACINTO PARAS & MANUELITO LUNA: SERIAL IMPEACHMENT HANDLERS

Jacinto "Jing" Paras has long been in the business of filing cases that align neatly with the priorities of whoever is in Malacañang. 

As a VACC figure and later a Duterte appointee, he helped push impeachment and complaints against Ombudsman Conchita Carpio-Morales, Chief Justice Maria Lourdes Sereno, Comelec Chair Andres Bautista, and Senator Antonio Trillanes IV — all critics or thorns in the side of the Duterte administration.

Paras later became Presidential Adviser on Political Affairs under Duterte, formalizing his role in political lawfare. 

His filing history shows a pattern: using impeachment and complaint mechanisms primarily against perceived enemies of his patrons, not as a consistent stand against corruption.

Manuelito Luna, another signatory, followed a similar path as a VACC lawyer and commissioner of the Presidential Anti-Corruption Commission under Duterte. 

He helped build and file complaints against Sereno and Bautista, fronting allegations of "betrayal of public trust" and ill-gotten wealth, while remaining quiet on abuses widely reported within the Duterte administration itself.

These are two of the men now invoking "betrayal of public trust" and "culpable violation of the Constitution" against Marcos Jr.

🟥  FERDINAND TOPACIO: LAWYER OF THE POWERFUL AND ACCUSED

Ferdinand Topacio has built a career representing some of the most controversial and powerful figures in Philippine politics and business. He has served as counsel for Gloria Macapagal Arroyo in electoral sabotage and plunder-related cases, and for Joseph Estrada in a multibillion-peso tax case.

He represents religious leader Apollo Quiboloy as the pastor faces human trafficking and abuse investigations in the Philippines and abroad. He also represents POGO figure Cassandra Ong, who is linked to a large illegal online gambling and trafficking case, pushing narratives that frame government actions as persecution while downplaying the gravity of the accusations.

As head of the VACC legal panel, Topacio has been part of the same circle that weaponizes "crime" and "corruption" cases against political opponents while defending entrenched power.

He now stands as one of the main legal brains behind the impeachment complaint accusing Marcos Jr. of systemic corruption, illegal drug use, and constitutional violations.

🟥  WHEN EVERYONE HAS A RAP SHEET

On paper, their complaint paints Marcos as a president who institutionalized systemic corruption and patronage through the "BBM parametric formula," tolerated ghost flood-control projects, failed to veto unconstitutional items in four years' worth of budgets, renewed Malampaya on questionable terms, imported excessive rice, used illegal drugs, and plotted Duterte's arrest and transfer to the ICC. 

They invoke big phrases: culpable violation of the Constitution, graft and corruption, betrayal of public trust, high crimes.

Those allegations deserve scrutiny on their own. But you cannot ignore who is reciting them:

A former Cabinet secretary and lawmaker tied to a major corruption scandal (NBN–ZTE), controversial mining reversals and deals, ivermectin misinformation, the ABS-CBN shutdown, and a flood-control "witness" whose affidavit and whereabouts became a mess. 

A political kingpin who admitted in court to being a jueteng bagman delivering hundreds of millions to a president, was named in illegal gambling probes, publicly admitted beating his partner and her alleged lover, and now faces plunder complaints over land deals. 

Two operators whose impeachment and complaint work under Duterte lined up consistently against his critics, not against corruption wherever it appeared. 

A lawyer whose client list is a gallery of presidents, power brokers, and accused traffickers, all needing protection from accountability.

It's an impeachment story, yes. But it's also something else: a snapshot of a political culture where "corruption" is a word everyone is willing to use, and almost no one is willing to own.

Marcos’ alleged sins sit on one side: the BBM parametric formula, flood control ghost projects, unconstitutional budget items, Malampaya renewal, rice importation, ICC, alleged drug use.

On the other side sit the case files and public admissions attached to the men trying to impeach him: NBN–ZTE, mining reversals and deals, ivermectin, ABS‑CBN shutdown, jueteng money, domestic violence complaints, plunder and graft raps, weaponized impeachments, and a “surprise” witness whose affidavit and whereabouts don’t hold up.

At some point, you stop asking who’s the most righteous in the room and start noticing how many are holding stones with dirty hands.

🟥 THE FLOOD CONTROL MESS DIDN'T START WITH BBM

If you follow the paper trail, the "flood control scandal" that everyone is now hanging on Marcos' neck is a system that predates him. 

Contractors, shell companies, copy-paste project descriptions, recycled designs — these patterns were already embedded in DPWH years before Marcos stood on the SONA stage and claimed 5,000+ flood control projects as an achievement. 

By 2025, an independent commission was already saying outright: the anomalies span Aquino, Duterte, and Marcos years. 

The audit Marcos ordered showed P100 billion in flood-control money going to just 15 contractors, and hundreds of billions' worth of projects without clear structures or with identical specs in different locations — a sign of a machine that had been running long before he got there.

Some of the names tied to controversial flood-control contractors surfaced as early as the Duterte years. 

There are reports and testimony pointing out that the Discaya group, now a star of the Blue Ribbon hearings, was already getting multiple big Build Build Build flood control packages under Duterte, with "ghostlike" results being whispered about long before Marcos formed the Independent Commission for Infrastructure.

So when the Defensor group acts like they suddenly "discovered" flood control corruption under Marcos, the obvious question is: where were you when Duterte was flooding DPWH with money?

🟥  IF THEY WERE SERIOUS, WHY ONLY NOW?

That's the part that's very irritating to me.

Duterte (and by extension, Mark Villar being the DPWH head) presided over record infrastructure budgets. Flood-control allocations surged under his term, and many of the contractors now under investigation were already fattening themselves in the Build Build Build era. 

If Defensor, Paras, Luna, and Topacio were truly moved by conscience about DPWH anomalies, they had six full Duterte years — plus the Pharmally years, plus the "DDS versus COA" years — to speak and file.

Instead, Paras and Luna spent those years filing cases against Sereno, Bautista, Morales, Trillanes — all critics or obstacles to Duterte. 

Topacio was defending Duterte allies and clients like Arroyo and Quiboloy, not building impeachment cases against the sitting president who controlled those flood-control billions. 

Defensor himself was nowhere near this level of moral outrage when the DPWH machine was being fed under Duterte. 

The flood-control "reformist" version of him only shows up after Marcos starts poking into congressional pork and contractors and after the Marcos–Duterte alliance begins to crack.

So the question practically asks itself: if the rot in DPWH goes back to Duterte, why is the impeachment happening only now, and only against Marcos?

🟥  IMPEACHMENT AS A POLITICAL WEAPON, NOT A MORAL ONE

I get that impeachment is technically a constitutional accountability tool. I also get that in practice, it's a blunt political weapon. 

You don't file it when your own president is in power and you're part of the inner circle; you file it when the alliances have changed, when your camp wants leverage or revenge, or when you're building narratives for the next election cycle.

The timeline of the flood-control scandal makes that obvious.

July 28, 2025: Marcos, in his SONA, publicly scolds officials over flood-control corruption and orders the full list of projects in the last three years made public.

August–October 2025: the commission he created starts naming contractors, tracing money flows, and pointing to massive concentration of funds in a handful of firms; subsequent updates implicate congressmen, DPWH officials, and even well-connected local players.

November 2025 onward: graft, plunder, corruption, and tax-evasion complaints begin to be filed, assets are frozen, and the investigation starts reaching closer to political heavyweights — including people linked to both Marcos and Duterte factions.

Then, January 2026: suddenly you have three impeachment attempts. Makabayan's complaint reads like a long, detailed charge sheet built around the BBM parametric formula, budget documents, and on-record admissions from people like Zaldy Co in previous hearings.

The Defensor–Singson–Paras–Topacio–Luna complaint, by contrast, is overloaded with everything and the kitchen sink — drugs, Malampaya, ICC, DPWH, rice importation — but light on specifics and filed after a stronger, earlier complaint had already been accepted, which triggers the one-year bar on new impeachments.

You can feel the difference in intent just by reading them. One is clearly designed to survive legal scrutiny; the other looks designed to score political points, produce soundbites, and ride the flood-control outrage without real expectation it will prosper.

🟥  MAKABAYAN VS. THE "GUNGGONG" COMPLAINT

That's the contrast that matters for me.

The Makabayan bloc took a narrow lane: they focused on the BBM parametric formula — the Baselined-Balanced-Managed scheme whose initials literally match Marcos' nickname — and argued that this formula institutionalized a new kind of pork, embedding presidential and congressional preferences into DPWH allocations. 

They cited DPWH policy documents, budget timelines, and on-record statements from Zaldy Co, including his own admission that he facilitated around P100 billion in insertions and that these insertions came after meetings where DBM officials said "tapos na ang meeting kay Presidente, may ipapasok na projects sa bicam."

Agree or disagree with their politics, the complaint shows work: specific documents, named policies, dates, amounts, and direct links between presidential decisions and corrupt structures. 

If, like me,  you're a citizen trying to understand how budget games become corruption, you'll actually learn something reading Makabayan's filing.

Then you look at the Defensor group's impeachment: a scattershot list of almost every possible impeachable ground, tied to a mix of partly-public allegations, half-formed narratives (like the Guteza saga), and political grievances — all coming from people with very thick case files of their own.

Given a choice, most serious readers will gravitate to the complaint that reads like an autopsy of a system, not a press conference dressed up as a constitutional instrument. 

If you really want to understand how BBM allegedly weaponized DPWH and the budget, you start with Makabayan's documents, not with the "gung-gong" complaint filed by a crew who discovered their moral outrage only after the flood-control scandal started splashing on their own networks.

🟥  THE REAL TAKEAWAY

When you zoom out, the story isn't just "Marcos versus his accusers." It's that the same DPWH corruption machine ran through Aquino, Duterte, and Marcos; that Marcos only moved when the floods, the numbers, and the politics forced his hand; and that the loudest impeachment warriors now were completely fine when the machine was feeding their own camp.

Impeachment in this context is less about cleaning house and more about positioning for the next round — who gets to claim the mantle of "anti-corruption," who gets to frame the narrative before the 2025 and 2028 races, who gets to say "kami ang nag-impeach."

That's why it makes sense to treat these complaints differently. 

You read Makabayan's filing for what it reveals about the BBM formula, pork, and how the presidency can engineer kickback systems.

You read the Defensor group's complaint with their own scandals and silences in mind — not to dismiss every accusation they make, but to remember that in Philippine politics, the people shouting "corrupt" are very often just describing a mirror.

"Let he who is without sin cast the first stone." - John 8:7

🟥 SARA DUTERTE IS A LIVE PR CASE STUDY

Every time I see Sara Duterte planting trees, giving out school bags, smiling with kids for the cameras, I’m reminded of the crisis communication manuals and image repair case studies I’ve been reading for years. 

The moves look familiar. The timing looks familiar. The messaging looks familiar.

Sara Duterte is under impeachment, facing plunder and graft complaints, entangled in confidential fund scandals, and on public record threatening to have the sitting president assassinated if anything happens to her. 

At the same time, she is being positioned as a 2028 presidential frontrunner, backed by strong survey numbers, regional machinery, and a very visible “good works” campaign wrapped in trees, school bags, and smiling photo ops.

The focus here is Sara, but the move itself is bigger than her. 

If you pay close attention to this pattern, you start seeing the same formula on other politicians—iba lang ang props, iba lang ang hashtag, pero pareho ang script.

That tension—that gap between the paper trail and the Instagram feed—is exactly where political PR lives.

🟥 I’M NOT PR, BUT I’VE BEEN WATCHING

Just to be clear about where this is coming from: I’m not a PR practitioner. 

Wala akong agency, wala akong client. I don’t claim to be an expert in public relations or crisis management. 

What I have are years of curiosity, a pile of readings, and a habit of noticing when the same moves get recycled.

I’ve been fascinated by image rehabilitation for years—especially how it’s used on politicians with baggage. 

I’ve spent a lot of time reading crisis communications playbooks, image restoration theory, and case studies on how public figures dig themselves out of scandals. 

So what I’m sharing here are patterns I see lining up, not insider trade secrets.

And when you put those frameworks beside what Sara is doing now, the pattern is almost textbook. Once you see it with her, you start noticing that the same pattern quietly appears every time a governor’s family is caught in a procurement scam, or a senator gets tagged in pork, or a mayor is linked to a killing and suddenly reinvents himself as a “family man” and “advocate.”

🟥 THE STANDARD IMAGE REHAB PLAYBOOK

Most serious PR and crisis comms guides say the same thing in different language. 

For damaged politicians, there are a few standard levers that keep showing up.

1. Manage the scandal’s “life cycle”  
You don’t actually resolve the issue right away; you manage time and attention.

Step 1.1: Face the cameras hard at the start. Give long, controlled interviews, say you’ll answer everything, appear “transparent.”  

Step 1.2: Once you’ve dumped enough words into the public sphere, you declare the topic “napag-usapan na ’yan.” From that point on, any further questions are “rehash,” “politically motivated,” “paninira.”  

The goal is to burn out the story. Media needs new angles; if you stop feeding them and refuse to comment, most outlets eventually move on. The scandal is technically unresolved, but ambient noise replaces active coverage.

2. Signal remorse without admitting guilt  
There’s a whole body of work on “mortification” in image repair, but in politics it becomes selective mortification.

- You apologize for “hurt feelings” or “mistakes” in judgment, not for crimes.  
- You talk about “learning,” “becoming better,” “listening more.”  
- You frame investigations as something you welcome “to clear my name,” which sounds cooperative while you still stonewall on specifics.  

Voters don’t need a court’s standard of proof. They just need an emotional sense that the person “owned up” somehow. That vague sense becomes cover for supporters who want a reason to stay.

3. Bolstering: drown the bad with staged good  
This is the part that feels very familiar watching Sara now.

In the literature, it’s called bolstering: you aggressively highlight good traits, good deeds, and “service” to offset the negative event. You don’t erase the scandal; you smother it under a mountain of supposedly unrelated kindness.

Look at the pattern:

- The “PagbaBAGo: A Million Learners and Trees” campaign: a combo of giving out bags with school supplies and dental kits while planting trees with kids and local officials.  

- Milestone events like planting the “one-millionth tree” under the Office of the Vice President’s program, complete with speeches about climate resilience and the next generation.  

- Nationwide roll-outs where tree planting and bag distribution happen simultaneously in Luzon, Visayas, and Mindanao.  

On paper, these are education and environment initiatives. 

In a crisis dictionary, this is reputational offset: high-visibility, high-photo-value events that say “I’m working for the children, I care about the environment, I’m doing something real.”

The timing matters. These kinds of campaigns become more intense and more publicized as legal and political blows get heavier. 

Every headline about confidential funds or plunder complaints now has to compete with feel-good coverage of seedlings and smiling students.

4. Wrap it in a grand narrative  
Separate from the events themselves is the story constantly attached to them.

- “Pagbabago sa Bawat Pilipino.”  
- “We’re planting trees for our children.”  
- “We hit one million trees in half the time because of volunteers and partners.”  

These are not just captions; they’re narrative anchors. The politician stops being “the official under investigation” and becomes “the leader on a mission.” 

When journalists or critics bring up scandals, the voting public doesn’t see it as oversight—they see it as villains attacking the hero in the story they’ve already bought into.

5. Attack the accuser and reframe the scandal  
Another standard move is “attacking the accuser” and “reducing offensiveness.”

In practice:

- Cast complainants as bitter, politically motivated, linked to rival camps.  
- Paint investigations as “weaponization” of institutions.  
- Frame all criticism as part of a larger conspiracy: “gustong pabagsakin,” “takot sila sa akin para sa 2028,” “ayaw nila sa taong lumalaban para sa masa.”  

You don’t have to disprove every allegation. You just need to give supporters a story that lets them ignore or reinterpret the details.

6. Compensation and side-door “correction”  
Sometimes, when the damage is serious, the playbook suggests some form of compensation or corrective action—again, without legal admission.

That can look like:

- Returning or reallocating some funds quietly, then later saying systems have been “tightened.”  
- Resigning from a cabinet post (DepEd) while staying as vice president, then moving aggressively into “non-controversial” advocacy work to show supposed repentance and refocus.  

It sends the signal: “I have already paid some price, I have corrected course.” Whether the move is true and with substance becomes secondary once the scandal settles.

The point isn't to solve issues, it's to make you look like you are doing something. 

🟥 OTHER STRATEGIES USED TO WASH DIRTY IMAGES

Beyond those big moves, there are smaller, repeatable tricks that show up across scandals, across countries.

1. Family and faith rebranding  
Suddenly there are more photos with children, spouses, parents, and more coverage inside churches or prayer rallies.

- Feature stories about being a “devoted” parent, “loving” son or daughter.  
- Strategic attendance at mass, worship nights, or religious festivals, always with cameras close.  

The subtext: “Whatever you heard, look at me with my family and my God. Would a truly bad person be like this?”

One recent example people brought up a lot online: the Chiz Escudero kare-kare video. 

Right after his name started getting dragged into flood control and corruption conversations, out comes a reel of him cooking kare-kare with his daughter—father-daughter bonding in a spotless kitchen. It’s simple, it’s charming, it’s domestic. 

And it shifts the conversation from “senator under scrutiny” to “mabait na tatay na marunong magluto.”

Bong Go has been running this play for years in his own style. 

There are reels of him in “ninong duties,” carrying a baby at a baptism, posing with toddlers and families in relief missions. The framing is always kuya, ninong, tito of the people. 

This runs beside allegations about cornering government projects for his family. The infant in his arms becomes a visual counterweight to the documents with his name on them.

2. Charity as shield  
This is different from one big signature program. This is a stream of small, highly visible donations.

- Scholarships, wheelchairs, dialysis support, burial assistance posts with “thank you” messages.  
- Relief goods with the politician’s name larger than the government agency’s logo.  

Each act becomes a mini-shield. 

When scandals surface, the online response is ready: “Eh ikaw ba, ilang tao na natulungan mo?” 

In Bong Go’s case, the “Ready Set Go” branding on relief operations blurred the line between public funds and personal generosity—every sack of rice became part of his PR armor.

3. Humanizing interviews and controlled vulnerability  
The media strategy shifts to “soft” sit-downs.

- Talk shows where they cry, talk about childhood wounds, unfair treatment, family struggles.  
- Questions pre-agreed, topics pre-screened, but framed as “no holds barred.”  

This creates emotional shortcuts. People who were uneasy about the allegations feel guilty for being “too harsh” once they’ve seen the tears.

4. Issue hijacking  
They attach themselves to a trending concern that has nothing to do with their scandal.

- Mental health, climate change, farmers’ rights, jeepney phase-out, etc.  
- They show up at the rallies, the press conferences, the summits, and position themselves as the most vocal champion.  

It works because people’s brains link them with the issue: “siya yung tumulong sa ganitong cause,” which helps push aside whatever they’re actually being investigated for.

5. Surrogate deployment  
When it’s too risky to be everywhere, you let other people do the talking.

- Lawyers, spokespersons, “friends,” influencers, bishops, pastors, or celebrities defending them on air and online.  
- Fan pages and “organic” supporters repeating the same lines: “innocent until proven guilty,” “let the court decide,” “trial by publicity.”  

The principal stays above the fray, looking “presidential” and “focused on work,” while the surrogates do the mud-wrestling.

6. Selective transparency  
They release some documents, do some “open” events, but on their own terms.

- They show a sanitized breakdown of expenses while keeping the most sensitive items hidden behind “security” or “classification.”  
- They choose friendly forums where no one has the time or expertise to interrogate the numbers.  

The point is not full disclosure. The point is to be able to say “nagpaliwanag na ako” and shame anyone who keeps asking.

Once you notice these moves with one politician, you start spotting them everywhere. 

A mayor caught on audio making threats suddenly appears at women’s rights events. 

A senator tagged in pork gets a new weekly relief project and starts doing teary interviews about his humble beginnings. 

A congressman named in a drug matrix becomes very visible in anti-drug campaigns and rehab center visits.

This is why it’s important to pay attention now, while Sara’s case is unfolding in real time. 

The specific scandals change, the personalities change, but the rehabilitation patterns repeat.

🟥 HOW SARA’S “GOOD DEEDS” FIT THE TEMPLATE

The PagbaBAGo program is a perfect example of bolstering, charity-as-shield, and narrative work operating together.

Tree-planting plus bag distribution does a few things at once:

- It puts her physically in communities—hugging kids, talking to parents, shoveling soil. That generates organic-looking content for local media and supporters’ Facebook feeds.  
- It positions her as someone who cares about both education and the environment, two “safe” issues that cut across political lines.  
- It allows every positive headline—“VP leads planting of one-millionth tree,” “OVP reaches one million learners”—to sit on the same timeline as headlines about confidential funds and plunder complaints.  

To a supporter scrolling on their phone, the emotional weight of “one million bags and one million trees” is easier to hold onto than the abstract numbers of “₱612.5 million in confidential funds” or “₱254.898 million in ghost expenses.” 

The rehab move is not to erase the numbers—it’s to out-feel them.

The structure of the campaign matches what crisis manuals recommend:

- Repetition over time, not one-off events  
- Clear, emotionally positive metrics (“one million learners,” “one million trees”)  
- Visuals that keep the principal front and center but framed as servant, not queen  
- Messaging that links individual acts (giving a bag, planting a tree) to a larger cause (“future generations,” “climate action”)  

If you were hired to clean up the image of a scandal-tainted politician, this is the sort of program you’d design. And once you know that, you start asking: which other politicians suddenly discovered their love for charity right after a scandal broke?

🟥 THE DIGITAL AMPLIFIER: TROLLS AS DISTRIBUTION

Offline events are only half the job. The other half is distribution, and that’s where Philippine troll farms enter.

We already know there’s a full-blown industry where people are paid above-average wages to run multiple fake accounts, pump up likes and shares, and swarm comment sections. Political PR firms tap these networks the way old campaigns tapped ward leaders: as organized ground troops, except this time online.

In an image rehab context, troll operations can:

- Mass-like and share every “positive” Sara Duterte story, pushing it into more feeds.  
- Copy-paste identical pro-Sara talking points under any critical post, creating the illusion of broad spontaneous support.  
- Attack journalists, whistleblowers, and even ordinary commenters who bring up confidential funds, plunder complaints, or the assassination threat—often with insults, accusations of bias, or threats.  

The digital work is designed to support the offline rehab strategy.

When she plants the “one-millionth tree,” trolls and aligned pages make sure that story trends in the right circles. 

When she hands out bags at a school, their content turns that into proof of “genuine concern for the youth” and “evidence” that funds are going somewhere good, whether or not that matches audit findings.

At the same time, any post mentioning ₱612.5 million in confidential funds, the ₱8 billion overpriced laptops, or ₱7 billion in unliquidated cash advances is flooded with comments saying: “Puro intriga,” “Pinupolitika lang,” “Mas marami siyang natulungan,” “Mas okay siya kaysa sa iba.” 

The intended effect is exhaustion—people get tired of wading through the noise and tune out the criticism.

And again, this is not just about her. 

Once you recognize the troll pattern around her name, you can start recognizing which other names travel with similar digital protection: same style of comments, same timing, same copy-pasted lines.

🟥 THE POINT OF CALLING IT OUT

None of this is accidental. 

This isn’t “she happens to like trees” and “oh, she also has cases.” 

It looks very much like deliberate use of known image rehabilitation strategies: manage the scandal timeline, perform controlled remorse, flood the zone with visible good works, wrap everything in an emotional narrative, attack accusers, then amplify it all with a professionalized online machine.

The reason to break this down is not to say PR is evil or that politicians should never do public service again after a scandal. It is to give people a mental checklist. 

So when the next politician under investigation suddenly launches a scholarship fund, a feeding program, a “listening tour,” a cooking video with their kid, or a “reconciliation concert,” something in your head goes: wait, I’ve seen this movie.

Sara Duterte just happens to be the clearest live example right now. But the formula she is benefiting from is already being used, quietly and repeatedly, by many others. 

The more people can see the pattern, the harder it becomes for anyone—Duterte, Marcos, Escudero, Bong Go, or whoever comes next—to wash a dirty record with photo ops and hashtags and call it redemption.

Have you spotted any of these PR moves lately—who was doing it, and what exactly were they doing? Tell me the story in the comments.

🟥 MARCOS FACES THREE IMPEACHMENT COMPLAINTS—AND THE FIRST ONE MIGHT BLOCK THE OTHERS

President Ferdinand "Bongbong" Marcos Jr. got hit with his first impeachment complaint on January 19, 2026. Atty. Andre de Jesus filed it, endorsed by Rep. Jernie Nisay of Pusong Pinoy party-list. 

It runs 14-15 pages and covers six allegations: ordering Duterte's "kidnapping" and ICC surrender, drug addiction, constitutional violations over unprogrammed appropriations, kickbacks from budget insertions and ghost flood control projects, creating the Independent Commission for Infrastructure to shield corrupt allies, and general betrayal of public trust.

Two more complaints came on January 22. The Bayan/Makabayan coalition filed a 37-page complaint backed by documented evidence of ₱545.6 billion in flood control corruption. 

ACT Teachers Rep. Antonio Tinio, Gabriela Rep. Sarah Elago, and Kabataan Rep. Renee Co endorsed it. 

Then, a third complaint surfaced filed by Attorney Manuelito Luna and other legal professionals, though details remain limited.

The problem: once the first complaint reaches the House Committee on Justice, a one-year constitutional bar activates. 

In short, no second  or third complaint can proceed until January 2027. 

🟥 THE CONSTITUTIONAL LOOPHOLE

Article XI, Section 3(5) of the 1987 Constitution says "No impeachment proceedings shall be initiated against the same official more than once within a period of one year." 

The Supreme Court has ruled that "initiated" means when the complaint lands with the Justice Committee, not when someone files it.

This creates an obvious vulnerability. 

A weak complaint reaching the committee first blocks a stronger one filed days later, regardless of evidence. 

Constitutional scholars have explicitly documented this exploit: "supporters of a vulnerable official can file a weak, flawed, or unconstitutional complaint, thereby using up the quota and protecting that official from impeachment for that year."

The Supreme Court had already addressed this problem. 

In July 2025, it ruled VP Sara Duterte's impeachment unconstitutional because the House archived three complaints to fast-track a fourth. 

The court found manipulation of complaint timing violated the one-year bar and due process.

Now the question sits unanswered: if archiving complaints to accelerate one is unconstitutional, what about rapidly processing a weak complaint to block stronger ones? 

House rules provide no guidance on the Secretary General's discretion in timing complaint transmittal.

🟥 WHY THE FIRST COMPLAINT LOOKS DELIBERATELY WEAK

Nearly every lawmaker who reviewed the first complaint called it deficient.

Rep. Edgar Erice (Caloocan, Deputy Minority Leader): It's "dead on arrival," containing "no evidence other than news articles." "Basura, hilaw na hilaw" (trash, very premature). Can easily be dismissed.

Rep. Zia Alonto Adiong (Lanao del Sur, Deputy Majority Leader): Weak and bound to fail. Seems based on hearsay. Made for media headlines than serious constitutional examination. Lacks substantial evidence.

Rep. Jonathan Keith Flores (Bukidnon, House Justice Committee Vice Chair): He asked directly whether the effort was "genuinely against the President or an effort to stop others from initiating a legitimate impeachment complaint."

Even Malacañang sent mixed signals, calling the complaint "not good for the economy" while downplaying its significance—an inconsistent response that suggested awareness of its protective function.

🟥 THE ENDORSER'S CORRUPTION PROBLEM

Rep. Jett Nisay endorsed the complaint. 

He's under investigation for the same flood control corruption the complaint alleges against Marcos.

Nisay owns JVN Construction, which secured ₱73 million in public works contracts (2021-2022). 

In November 2025—two months before endorsing the impeachment—the Independent Commission for Infrastructure (ICI) and DPWH recommended investigating him for flood control anomalies. 

He's one of seven House members implicated in the scandal alongside former Rep. Elizaldy Co.

A lawmaker accused of corruption in flood control projects endorses an impeachment complaint citing flood control corruption. 

But the complaint is so weak it effectively shields the president from accountability for that very scandal. 

The strategic value to both Nisay (deflecting from his own culpability) and Marcos (blocking stronger complaints) jumps out immediately.

🟥 HOW THE FIRST COMPLAINT GOT SPECIAL TREATMENT

Normal processing (VP Sara Duterte case): Complaints filed December 2024 sat archived for two months before a fourth complaint filed February 5, 2025.

Marcos first complaint processing: Filed Monday, January 19. Transmitted to Speaker's office by Wednesday, January 21—during congressional recess (House reconvenes January 26).

House Secretary General Garafil confirmed transmitting the complaint "as per established protocol," yet this rapid processing contrasts sharply with how the initial Duterte complaints were handled.

Rep. Erice directly warned about this timing manipulation: "If they expedite the complaint and effectively block other impeachment actions, then that indicates the motive... 

The true intentions will become evident depending on how the House proceeds with this impeachment complaint."

🟥 WHEN THE SECOND COMPLAINT HIT A WALL

The Bayan/Makabayan coalition tried filing their 37-page, evidence-backed complaint on January 22. Garafil was in Taiwan receiving an award. Executive Director Jose Marmoi Salonga refused to accept the complaint, claiming he "had no authority."

Complainants resorted to "substitute service," leaving documents on a desk. 

The Makabayan bloc claimed the complaint was "deemed filed" under House rules.

Former Bayan Muna Rep. Neri Colmenares (a practicing lawyer) argued: "The Constitution mentions no requirement for the Secretary General's personal presence." 

Kabataan Rep. Renee Co added: "Nowhere in the rules does it indicate that the Secretary General's personal presence is required."

The contrast is stark. 

The first complaint received immediate personal attention from Garafil during recess. 

The second complaint—filed with substantially more documentation—got rejected on technicalities when congressional staff could have accepted it.

🟥 WHAT'S ACTUALLY UNDER THE MICROSCOPE

Understanding what the preemptive complaint might be protecting reveals the stakes. The flood control scandal involves billions.

Total flood control budget (July 2022-May 2025): ₱545.6 billion.

Concentration: Only 15 of 2,409 accredited contractors got ₱100 billion—18% of total budget.

Estimated kickback rates: 25-30% according to Senate investigations, leaving only 30-40% for actual construction.

2025 budget insertions: ₱100 billion allegedly ordered by Marcos, with ₱25 billion in kickbacks.

The Bayan complaint centers on the "BBM Parametric Formula"—the budget allocation mechanism used 2022-2026. 

Page 6 of the DPWH document states: "The priorities set by national government leaders and the legislature will also be factored in."

The Makabayan bloc argues this phrasing—"priorities of the leaders" rather than "priorities of the administration"—explicitly institutionalizes individual patronage rather than institutional policy. This transforms infrastructure planning into a formalized pork barrel system.

Former Rep. Elizaldy Co (fled to the United States): Alleged in video testimony that Marcos personally ordered ₱100 billion in budget insertions during bicameral conference committee. Claimed DPWH officials told him Marcos receives 25% "SOP" (kickbacks). Stated his former aide, Orly Guteza, delivered "suitcases filled with cash" to Malacañang.

Commission on Audit findings (September 2025): DPWH officials and contractors in Bulacan face potential charges of graft, malversation, and falsification over ₱360 million in flood control projects. Multiple projects had substandard work, ghost projects, and hazardous infrastructure.

Anti-Money Laundering Council action (September 2025): Secured court approval to freeze 135 bank accounts and 27 insurance policies of individuals accused of corruption in flood control projects.

🟥 WHY THE NUMBERS DON'T WORK ANYWAY

Conviction requires one-third of all House members—106 votes. Right now, fewer than 10 members support impeachment. Even if the complaint gets past the Justice Committee, the math collapses.

The National Unity Party explicitly rejected any impeachment move against Marcos, with NUP chairman and Deputy Speaker Ronaldo Puno stating: "I don't see any grounds for filing an impeachment case against the President." With NUP alone commanding 33 votes, and Lakas-CMD's 111 seats, Marcos maintains comfortable insulation.

🟥 THE POLITICS ARE TRANSPARENT

Rep. Paolo "Pulong" Duterte (Davao City) didn't mince words: "Scripted distraction... Mga impeachment cases na ganito ay walang ibang purpose kundi ang idepensa ang isang impeachable official laban sa mga totoong impeachment cases" (Impeachment cases like this have no other purpose than to defend an impeachable official against real impeachment cases).

South China Morning Post framed it plainly: "The filing could trigger procedural limits that would block more substantive impeachment bids... a weak case that, once taken up by the House of Representatives, could trigger a constitutional one-year bar on further impeachment bids."

Rep. Edgar Erice's explicit hope: "Sana hindi mapigilan nitong impeachment complaint na ito yung mga susunod pang mga impeachment complaint... 'Pag referred na sa committee on justice eh wala nang ibang pwedeng makapag file ng impeachment complaint" (I hope this impeachment complaint won't be able to block succeeding impeachment complaints... Because once this has been referred to the committee on justice, nobody else can file another impeachment complaint).

Bayan President Renato Reyes: Expressed hope their verified complaint would be "delivered to the Speaker and included in the agenda, rather than being sidelined in favor of what many consider a feeble first complaint meant to protect the President."

🟥 WHAT COMES NEXT

Most likely: The Justice Committee dismisses the first complaint for lack of merit. If it's dismissed without referral to the full House, the one-year bar doesn't activate, keeping the window open for the Bayan/Makabayan complaint.

The wild card: When does the Justice Committee meet to evaluate the first complaint? If it happens before January 26 through special session, it could dismiss and close the preemption attempt. If it waits until regular session, the second complaint might gain standing.

The legal uncertainty: No clear precedent exists on whether substitute service applies to impeachment filing. House rules say complaints must go to the "Office of the Secretary General"—but don't specify personal receipt requirements.

Supreme Court wildcard: If preemption succeeds and blocks substantive complaints, affected parties could petition the Court, arguing grave abuse of discretion. 

The third complaint from attorney Manuelito Luna and other former officials sits in the same limbo. It exists on paper, but it’s trapped behind the one-year bar and the House gatekeeping.

The SC's July 2025 Duterte ruling showed willingness to intervene in impeachment procedures when constitutional violations occur. 

The Court has been reluctant to interfere with House internal procedures absent clear constitutional breach, though.

🟥 THE BIGGER PICTURE

The 1987 Constitution's framers built the one-year bar to stop harassment—to keep officials from getting pummeled with frivolous complaints every few weeks.

The Supreme Court made clear in 2025 (ruling on VP Duterte's case) that the rule was meant to stop repeated attacks, not give officials permanent protection from accountability.

Now it's protecting Marcos.

A weak complaint endorsed by a guy under investigation for flood control corruption blocks the real case from even being filed. The rule meant to shield officials from abuse became the weapon. The evidence is there—whistleblower testimony, COA findings, frozen accounts. Billions in flood control money that vanished. But procedure locks it out.

It's a constitutional failure.

Yet lawmakers said it out loud. Media called it. Civil society pushed back. The system caught itself. Whether that's enough to stop the blockade—that's the open question.

🟥 THE FLOOD CONTROL SCAM IS A MANUAL FOR PROTECTING BIG FISH

The flood control scam that spans the administrations of Duterte and Marcos Jr. is a textbook example of how the Philippine state protects its biggest political fish.

Not because it is the first or the worst, but because almost every layer of protection shows up here at once: in the budget room, in Senate hearings, inside the Ombudsman’s office, and in how powerful politicians surf over scandal and stay in place.

You don’t need the entire case file to see it. You just need to follow three levels: political shielding, institutional cushioning, and how senators named in the scam still come out safe.

🟥 LEVEL 1: POLITICAL SHIELDING

Under Duterte, DPWH was the poster boy of “Build, Build, Build.” Mark Villar, then secretary, bragged about thousands of flood control structures as proof that government was finally serious about disaster prevention. 

Flood projects became political capital: more walls, more ribbon cuttings, more tarps, more photo ops. Nobody in power wanted to ask how that spending culture warped incentives inside DPWH.

Marcos Jr. inherited that machine and pushed even more money through it. From 2022 to 2025, government poured around ₱545.64 billion into flood control projects. 

Later estimates pegged about ₱142 billion of that as possibly lost to corruption, dwarfing many post‑EDSA scandals in scale. Only 15 favored contractors got about ₱100 billion—18 percent of the entire flood control budget.

Then the testimonies started.

Former Ako Bicol Rep. Zaldy Co, who chaired the House appropriations committee, claimed that then–Speaker Martin Romualdez ordered him to deliver ₱2 billion per month in kickbacks from DPWH projects. 

He alleged that Marcos Jr. himself took a 25 percent cut—about ₱7 billion—from a ₱28‑billion insertion, and that Romualdez threatened to shoot him if he talked. 

Co’s own family companies reportedly bagged at least ₱15.7 billion in flood control contracts between 2022 and 2025.

Malacañang’s answer was confidence. Marcos promised that by Christmas 2025, “many will be arrested” for the flood control scam and “they will not enjoy a Merry Christmas.” 

The administration spoke of a relentless anti‑corruption drive and “no sacred cows.” The DILG teased that “big fish [are] coming soon” as some suspects entered the Witness Protection Program.

But by December 25, the reality was clear:

- Charged and detained: district‑level DPWH officials, mid‑level engineers, and contractors.  
- Facing asset freezes: contractors and alleged local operators, with billions in assets frozen by AMLC.  
- Still free: Marcos Jr., Romualdez, Co (now in hiding), dozens of lawmakers, and several senators tagged in testimony or investigative reports.  

A lawmaker summed it up bluntly: no big fish behind bars, despite the President’s promise. 

Instead, Marcos approved the 2026 budget with another huge flood package structurally similar to the corrupted 2025 funds. The apparatus wasn’t dismantled; it was rolled forward.

That gap between rhetoric and actual targets is the first shield. The outrage is loud. The arrests land on small actors. The big names stay insulated by time, process, and position.

🟥 LEVEL 2: INSTITUTIONAL CUSHIONING

If political shielding is about who gets promised to the public, institutional cushioning is about how “accountability” is managed from the inside.

Start with the Senate Blue Ribbon Committee.

Rodante Marcoleta entered the flood control hearings with a tough persona. 

There were subpoenas for absent contractors and strong language about going after masterminds. But when key people were in front of him, his choices told a different story.

He excused former DPWH Secretary Manuel Bonoan. After Bonoan said he had already resigned and no longer had the capacity to speak for DPWH, Marcoleta granted his request to be excused from the hearing. 

No deep questioning on command responsibility. No insistence that leadership remains accountable for what happened on their watch. 

A former secretary walked out of the room with minimal friction.

He comforted a colleague on record, albeit in jest, but still... 

In one hearing, Marcoleta told Senator Jinggoy Estrada “safe ka na” after contractor Curlee Discaya denied senatorial involvement. This was before all testimonies were in. 

Later, DPWH engineer Brice Ericson Hernandez would allege that Estrada and Senator Joel Villanueva got a 30‑percent cut from certain Bulacan flood projects. 

The comfort came early; the scrutiny came late, and mostly outside the Blue Ribbon frame.

He vouched for a collapsing “surprise witness.” Marcoleta presented Orly Regala Guteza as a surprise witness who supposedly delivered suitcases of cash to Zaldy Co and Romualdez. 

Guteza asked to enter Witness Protection, then skipped his DOJ appointment. 

The lawyer whose name appeared on his affidavit said he hadn’t notarized it. 

Instead of backing away, Marcoleta defended Guteza in media and called him credible, effectively lawyering for a witness whose story couldn’t survive basic checks.

He also spent (and continues to spend) unusual energy trying to frame Pacifico and Sarah Discaya—operators in ghost flood projects—as potential state witnesses, drawing criticism that he was being “too protective” of them. 

For a hearing supposedly chasing masterminds, the chair often looked like he was cushioning certain players instead of cornering them.

Eventually, leadership adjusted. 

Panfilo Lacson took over, and later Erwin Tulfo was named acting Blue Ribbon chair. 

Minority senators issued their own report, arguing the official probe stopped short of fully pinning down top officials. 

Lacson publicly dismissed a pro‑administration “minority report” by Imee Marcos and Marcoleta as lacking institutional weight and disrespecting the committee’s work. 

It was a polite way of saying: the first round didn’t do the job.

Now look at the Ombudsman.

In October 2025, Marcos appointed Jesus Crispin “Boying” Remulla—his former Justice Secretary and longtime political ally—as Ombudsman. 

The Ombudsman is the only office that can bring plunder and high‑level graft cases against top officials to court. 

Whoever sits there effectively decides which big fish ever see a courtroom.

Remulla’s record leans heavily toward protecting state agents. 

As Justice Secretary, he oversaw the dismissal of multiple “Bloody Sunday” cases, where activists were killed in police and military raids, leaving victims’ families without justice and reinforcing a pattern of impunity. 

As Ombudsman, he has junked counter‑charges against soldiers and police, often citing “lack of evidence” and the presumption that public officials act regularly and in good faith. 

Human rights groups say he has turned a constitutional watchdog into a fortress protecting the powerful, especially Marcos Jr.

When news broke that a congressman and a contractor had allegedly tried to bribe the Remulla brothers during the flood control probe, he publicized the story. 

But no solid case or arrests followed, with the bribe described as coming through emissaries and therefore just “hearsay.” 

The episode boosted his image as incorruptible without producing concrete accountability for the alleged bribe‑givers.

House leaders now call him “the man,” praise his “firm resolve,” and insist he be given space to work “without pressure.” 

In practice, that means giving a political ally broad discretion over which cases against big fish move, and which quietly die.

Between a Senate investigation that cushions key figures and an Ombudsman’s office headed by a friend of the President, institutional cushioning becomes visible. 

The processes that should be most dangerous to big fish are staffed and steered in ways that make those fish relatively safe.

🟥 LEVEL 3: SENATORS NAMED, SENATORS SAFE — VILLAR, ESTRADA, VILLANUEVA, ESCUDERO

The third level is where names and faces come in. 

Several sitting or recent senators have been publicly named in testimonies and reports on the flood control scandal. None are in jail. All remain politically active.

Start with Mark Villar.

As Duterte’s DPWH secretary, he oversaw the massive expansion of flood control projects under “Build, Build, Build” and became the infrastructure poster boy. 

Later, investigations found that companies tied to his relatives—like Motiontrade Development Corporation, owned by his uncle Christian Aguilar—bagged hundreds of millions in flood control contracts and billions more in other public works. 

The DOJ and Ombudsman confirmed they were examining whether Villar family business interests benefited from DPWH projects.

Former undersecretary Roberto Bernardo went further, testifying that Villar, Manuel Bonoan, and undersecretary Cathy Cabral were part of a kickback arrangement where budget insertions for flood control yielded commissions, with a cut allegedly going to Villar through his cousin Carlo Aguilar, cast as a bagman. 

Villar’s response: everything is baseless, he has nothing to hide, and he is ready to face any probe. He remains a senator, part of the institution supposedly investigating the very system he helped build.

Then there is the Estrada–Villanueva axis.

Brice Ericson Hernandez, a DPWH district engineer in Bulacan, testified that Senators Jinggoy Estrada and Joel Villanueva allegedly took about 30 percent in commissions from certain flood control projects in his area. 

Henry Alcantara, another former official, described how nearly ₱1 billion in projects were carved up, with a portion going to these senators. 

The Independent Commission for Infrastructure and the NBI recommended plunder, bribery, and graft complaints against them to the Ombudsman. 

Both senators deny the accusations. Both remain in office, doing committee work and weighing in on new budgets.

Estrada’s history makes this even heavier. 

He was jailed in 2014 over his alleged role in the PDAF scam, only to later return to the Senate after legal maneuvers and acquittals. 

Now his name is back in another multi‑billion‑peso scandal, and again, he sits on the “investigator” side of the table.

Joel Villanueva, who brands himself as a reformer and moral voice, now has to answer for testimonies that describe percentage cuts and commissions on projects that were supposed to protect communities from floods. His line is total denial. The system’s line is business as usual.

Chiz Escudero’s case looks different but fits the same pattern of survival.

He was ousted as Senate President in 2025 after reports showed that his top donor in the 2022 elections, Lawrence Lubiano, was president of Centerways Construction, a contractor that reportedly bagged over ₱5 billion in flood control projects, including dozens in Escudero’s home province of Sorsogon. 

Lubiano admitted to donating ₱30 million to Escudero’s campaign, insisting it was his own money and not sourced from government contracts. 

Escudero argued that the donation was properly reported and legal, and that contractors are not banned from donating.

The Comelec's Political Finance and Affairs Department cleared both Escudero and Lubiano in November 2025, ruling that Lubiano donated "in his personal capacity" and that there was no evidence the funds came from Centerways. 

The decision rested on the principle that Lubiano and his company have "separate juridical personality." 

Former Senator Leila de Lima immediately questioned whether the ruling "may have been short on diligence," pointing out that PFAD should have examined the ownership structure of Centerways and whether Lubiano wholly owns it—invoking the legal doctrine of "piercing the corporate veil." 

Citizens groups went further, calling on Comelec Chairman George Garcia to disclose a potential conflict of interest: Garcia had served as Escudero's lawyer during the 2022 elections, and his wife serves as deputy secretary for legislation in the Senate, a position she obtained under then-Senate President Escudero. 

Election lawyers warned the decision sets a "dangerous precedent," creating a loophole that allows contractors to funnel campaign donations through company officers acting "in personal capacity," effectively gutting Section 95 of the Omnibus Election Code.

Then came Bernardo’s testimony. 

The former DPWH undersecretary alleged that Escudero benefited from flood control budget insertions and that he delivered alleged kickbacks through businessman Maynard Ngu, the Cherry Mobile CEO who also served as Special Envoy to China. Bernardo claimed he handed Ngu at least ₱160 million, later saying the total reached ₱280 million in two tranches, supposedly representing commissions from ₱800 million worth of flood projects tied to Escudero. 

Ngu and Escudero both deny these claims. Ngu calls them lies. Escudero says he never dealt with Bernardo directly and is being made a scapegoat.

As of now, the Ombudsman says it is still building evidence and considering possible cases. Escudero is not behind bars. He remains a seasoned player in a Senate still dominated by familiar surnames.

Villar, Estrada, Villanueva, Escudero: different clans, different styles, different defenses. 

But all share one thing. 

In a scandal of this scale, they are named. 

They are investigated on paper. They deny everything. And the system, so far, has not made any of them pay in a way that ends their political lives.

🟥 WHY THIS MATTERS BEYOND ONE SCANDAL

It’s tempting to treat the flood control mess as another entry in a long list of corruption stories.

But this one is doing something else: it’s teaching in real time.

It shows how a spending culture under Duterte, built on hype and concrete, created fertile ground for a gigantic racket under Marcos Jr. Flood control became a bottomless justification: climate change, disaster risk, resilience. 

Nobody wanted to be the politician who said, “Wait, show me the contracts.”

It shows how a president can promise arrests and deliver only on small targets, while politically connected names remain resource persons, not defendants. 

The state performs anger on television and leniency in case folders.

It shows how a Senate committee can look aggressive in public yet consistently make choices—excusing Bonoan, comforting Estrada, propping up a shaky witness—that soften the blow for peers rather than sharpen it.

It shows how an Ombudsman with tight ties to the Palace can quietly decide whether explosive testimonies become live cases or dead letters, turning a constitutional safeguard into a gatekeeper for elite impunity.

It shows how senators named in sworn testimonies and investigative reports can remain in power, deny everything, and rely on time, process, and friendly institutions to outlast the public’s attention span.

“Protecting the big fish” isn’t a metaphor here. It’s a workflow.

Money is moved in bicameral rooms and budget insertions. Commissions are sliced off and passed upward. 

When the scam is exposed, the system offers up engineers and contractors to satisfy public anger. 

Hearings are held, statements are issued, a few accounts are frozen. 

Then the cases that truly matter enter a long tunnel of “further investigation,” “evidence‑building,” and “legal processes” overseen by people who owe their positions to the same political class that stands to lose the most.

By the time we find out how it ends, many Filipinos will already be bracing for the next flood, in the same barangays, along the same rivers, wondering how billions could have been spent on flood control and still leave them waist‑deep in water.
